verses.text: Fleeing for his life, David comes to the priest Ahimelech and, to get help, tells a lie about a secret royal mission. Even a man after God's heart, gripped by fear, resorts to deceit. Faith can falter; David's panic leads him into a falsehood that will cost others dearly.

Fleeing for his life, David comes to the priest Ahimelech and, to get help, tells a lie about a secret royal mission. Even a man after God's heart, gripped by fear, resorts to deceit. Faith can falter; David's panic leads him into a falsehood that will cost others dearly.

Fleeing for his life, David comes to the priest Ahimelech and, to get help, tells a lie about a secret royal mission. Even a man after God's heart, gripped by fear, resorts to deceit. Faith can falter; David's panic leads him into a falsehood that will cost others dearly.

The only bread is the holy shewbread, reserved for priests, and Ahimelech gives it to the hungry men. Jesus later points to this very act: mercy and human need may rightly bend ritual. God prizes compassion above mere ceremony, even with bread set apart for Him.

The only bread is the holy shewbread, reserved for priests, and Ahimelech gives it to the hungry men. Jesus later points to this very act: mercy and human need may rightly bend ritual. God prizes compassion above mere ceremony, even with bread set apart for Him.

The only bread is the holy shewbread, reserved for priests, and Ahimelech gives it to the hungry men. Jesus later points to this very act: mercy and human need may rightly bend ritual. God prizes compassion above mere ceremony, even with bread set apart for Him.

The narrator quietly notes that Doeg the Edomite, Saul's chief herdsman, was there that day, watching. A single hostile witness is present at David's lie. Small details, easily overlooked, can carry terrible consequences — sin done in haste is often seen by eyes we never noticed.

David asks for a weapon and is given the sword of Goliath, kept behind the ephod. 'There is none like that,' he says. The trophy of his greatest victory becomes his help in flight. God's past deliverances are given back to us as encouragements in present fear.

David asks for a weapon and is given the sword of Goliath, kept behind the ephod. 'There is none like that,' he says. The trophy of his greatest victory becomes his help in flight. God's past deliverances are given back to us as encouragements in present fear.

David flees to Gath — to the Philistines, of all places — but is recognized as the famous giant-slayer, and grows 'sore afraid.' Running from one danger, he stumbles into another. When we scheme our own escape apart from God, we often land in a worse corner.

David flees to Gath — to the Philistines, of all places — but is recognized as the famous giant-slayer, and grows 'sore afraid.' Running from one danger, he stumbles into another. When we scheme our own escape apart from God, we often land in a worse corner.

David flees to Gath — to the Philistines, of all places — but is recognized as the famous giant-slayer, and grows 'sore afraid.' Running from one danger, he stumbles into another. When we scheme our own escape apart from God, we often land in a worse corner.

To survive, David feigns madness, scrabbling at the gate and drooling, until Achish drives him out. The mighty man of faith is reduced to playing the fool. God preserves him even so — but the chapter shows how low fear can drag even the bravest, until faith revives.
